items.mistake: Using train accuracy alone to pick the best model
items.fix: Designate a validation primary metric (val/f1, val/rmse) and rank checkpoints by validation curves; keep train metrics for diagnostics only.
items.why: Train metrics measure fit on seen data— they improve with memorization even when generalization degrades.
items.example: Wrong: save model with highest train/accuracy. Right: save checkpoint with best val/f1 even if train/accuracy is lower.

items.mistake: Logging test metrics every epoch in the training loop
items.fix: Log test/* once after selecting the best validation checkpoint; keep test evaluation out of the live training dashboard.
items.why: Repeated test logging invites checkpoint selection on test curves— the same leakage as tuning hyperparameters on the test set.
items.example: Wrong: `for epoch: log(test/f1, ...)`. Right: after training, `log(test/f1, evaluate(test_set), tag=final)`.

items.fix: Adopt a team convention: split prefix + canonical name (val/loss, val/f1) and enforce it in shared logging helpers.
items.why: Experiment trackers cannot overlay curves or build leaderboards when the same metric has different keys in different runs.
items.example: Wrong: run1 logs `validation_f1`, run2 logs `val/f1`. Right: both log `val/f1` with hyperparameters distinguishing runs.

items.mistake: Plotting train and validation on different x-axes without documenting units
items.fix: Use one x-axis definition (global_step or epoch) for all series in a run; log the unit in run metadata.
items.why: Misaligned axes make curves incomparable and can hide that validation was evaluated less frequently than training loss.
items.example: Wrong: train/loss by step 1–10k, val/loss by epoch 1–50 on the same chart without conversion. Right: val/loss logged at the global_step when each epoch completes.
